themeBlurb: Paper 2 Booklet A (Visual Text 5m) + Booklet B (Cloze 15m, Editing 10m, Synthesis & Transformation 10m, Open-Ended 20m). The biggest mark pool in the entire exam.

comprehension.title: Comprehension Open-Ended (20m)
comprehension.blurb: ONE passage (narrative or non-narrative, 500-600 words), ~10 questions. RACE framework on every 2+ mark question.
keyQuestions: Is this question literal, inferential, vocabulary-in-context, language-use, author's purpose, or personal response?
Does my answer Restate, Answer, Cite (with exact quote), and Explain?
For language-use: have I named the technique AND explained the effect on the reader?
For personal-response: have I taken a clear stance AND anchored in text evidence?

glossary.definition: Restate, Answer, Cite Evidence, Explain. The standard structure for 2+ mark open-ended answers.
glossary.template: Use it on every inferential question.

glossary.definition: Asks 'how do you know' or 'what does this suggest'. Always supported with a text quote.
glossary.template: Marcus felt anxious; this is shown when 'his fingers trembled around the cup'.

glossary.definition: Asks about the effect of a phrase or technique. Answer in two parts: name the technique, explain the effect.
glossary.template: The simile 'as cold as ice' creates a sense of cruelty and distance.

glossary.definition: Asks 'do you agree' or 'what do you think'. Take a clear stance + anchor in text evidence.
glossary.template: Yes, I agree that Daniel should have told the truth. The text states 'a knot of guilt in his stomach', showing the silence was hurting him.

comprehension.learnHTML:
Why this topic matters

Comprehension Open-Ended is 20 marks. One passage — either narrative or non-narrative; the genre rotates between years. About 10 questions. Constructed-response (no options). With Cloze (15m) and S&T (10m), this is part of the 45-mark constructed-response pool in Booklet B — the densest mark cluster in the exam.

RACE — the most important framework

Use it for every answer worth 2 or more marks.

LetterMeaningWhat to write
RRestateRephrase the question as the start of your answer.
AAnswerGive the direct answer.
CCite EvidenceQuote from the text using quotation marks.
EExplainExplain HOW the quote supports your answer.

The six question types (quick sorter)

If stem says…It's a…Answer it by…
"According to the text…"LiteralFind it word-for-word. Quote exactly.
"How did / What did the character…?"InferentialRACE. Cite an exact quote that supports your inference.
"What does this word mean as used here?"Vocabulary-in-contextDefine using context clues. Never with the same root word.
"Why does the writer use this phrase / technique?"Language-useName the technique AND explain the effect. Always two parts.
"What is the main purpose of this paragraph?"Author's purposeExplain why the writer included it; link to the overall argument or story.
"Do you agree / What do you think?"Personal responseTake a clear stance + text evidence + your own reasoning.

RACE worked example

Question (2 marks): 'How did Marcus feel when he heard the news? Support your answer with evidence from the text.'

Weak answer (1 mark): 'He felt sad.'

Strong RACE answer (2 marks): 'Marcus felt devastated when he heard the news. This is shown when the text states "his knees buckled beneath him and his vision blurred with tears", suggesting that the news was so overwhelming that his body could not cope with the grief.'

Why it works:

  • R: 'Marcus felt devastated when he heard the news' (restates question).
  • A: 'devastated' (direct answer with a precise word).
  • C: 'his knees buckled beneath him and his vision blurred with tears' (exact quote in quotation marks).
  • E: 'suggesting that the news was so overwhelming that his body could not cope' (explains the quote).

Language-use answers — the two-part formula

Always two parts: (1) Name the TECHNIQUE, (2) Explain the EFFECT on the reader.

Template: "The writer uses [TECHNIQUE], comparing/showing [WHAT], to create [EFFECT] for the reader."

Common techniques to name:

  • Simile (using like/as): "as cold as ice", "like a wounded animal".
  • Metaphor (direct comparison): "His voice was thunder."
  • Personification (giving human qualities to non-human): "The sun smiled down."
  • Alliteration: "softly, silently, smoothly".
  • Onomatopoeia: "crash, sizzle, whisper".
  • Hyperbole: "I've told you a million times."
  • Repetition: "She waited, and waited, and waited."
  • Imagery: "the sharp smell of rain on dry earth".

Common effects to explain: creates urgency · suggests intimacy · evokes emotion · emphasises the point · builds anticipation · conveys atmosphere · creates a sense of menace · creates contrast.

Personal-response answers — the three-part formula

  1. Clear stance. 'Yes, I agree…' or 'No, I disagree…' — never fence-sit ('Maybe yes, maybe no').
  2. Text evidence. Anchor with a quote: 'The text states…'
  3. Your own reasoning. Add a line of personal logic that builds on the text.

Example: "Yes, I agree that Daniel should have told his parents the truth. In the text, Daniel describes feeling 'a knot of guilt in his stomach that would not go away', which shows that keeping the secret was causing him distress. Had he been honest, he would have felt relieved and his parents could have helped him resolve the situation."
comprehension.summaryHTML: Comprehension OE is 20 marks — one passage. The genre rotates between years (narrative or non-narrative). Full strategy in Comprehension_Strategy_Guide.md.
pslePrompts: Always use RACE for 2+ mark questions.
Quote exactly — use quotation marks. Examiners reward exact citation.
For 'do you agree' questions: take a clear stance, then anchor in text evidence.
For language-use: technique + effect on reader. Always two parts.
Never define a vocab word with its own root ('ravenous means to raven' = 0 marks).

commonTraps.wrong: "He was sad." (No quote, no explanation — for a 2-mark inferential question.)
commonTraps.fix: Use RACE: Restate + Answer with precise word + Cite exact quote + Explain how the quote supports.
commonTraps.why: Examiners cap at 1 mark without a citation. The 'C' in RACE is non-negotiable for inferential/personal-response questions.

commonTraps.wrong: Language-use answer that only names the technique: "It is a simile."
commonTraps.fix: Two parts: name the technique AND explain the effect on the reader. "The writer uses a simile to suggest X, creating a sense of Y for the reader."
commonTraps.why: Language-use questions are worth 2m each. Naming alone caps at 1m. The effect is half the marks.

commonTraps.wrong: Fence-sitting on personal-response questions: "Maybe yes, maybe no."
commonTraps.fix: Take a clear stance: "Yes, I agree…" or "No, I disagree…". Then anchor in text evidence.
commonTraps.why: Examiners reward conviction backed by evidence. Indecision signals you have not engaged with the text.

commonTraps.wrong: Defining vocab-in-context with the same root: "Ravenous means to raven."
commonTraps.fix: Use a different word or short paraphrase: "Ravenous means extremely hungry."
commonTraps.why: Defining with the same root word demonstrates you do not understand it. 0 marks.

commonTraps.wrong: Copying long stretches of the passage as your answer.
commonTraps.fix: Answer in your OWN words, then quote ONE short phrase as evidence (1–2 lines max).
commonTraps.why: Long quoting suggests you can't summarise. Examiners reward your synthesis + a precise quote, not transcript.

commonTraps.wrong: Ignoring the second part of a 2-part question ("How did X feel AND what did he do as a result?").
commonTraps.fix: Read the question twice. Number both parts. Answer BOTH in your response.
commonTraps.why: Missing one half of a 2-part question caps you at 1 mark even if the half you answered is perfect.

masteryCheckpoints: You can sort a question into one of the six types in 10 seconds (literal / inferential / vocab / language-use / purpose / personal).
You use RACE for every 2+ mark question — Restate, Answer directly, Cite with quotation marks, Explain how the quote supports.
For language-use questions, you name the technique AND explain the effect (never just one).
You anchor personal-response answers in specific text evidence — never just an opinion.
You quote SHORT phrases (1–2 lines max) rather than long passage extracts.
You use precise emotion vocabulary (devastated, bereft, elated) rather than vague (sad, happy, nice).
You answer BOTH halves of any 2-part question.

questions.question: Which answer best follows the RACE structure for the question 'How did Marcus feel when he heard the news? Support your answer with evidence.' (2 marks)?
options: Marcus felt very, very sad about the news.
Marcus felt devastated when he heard the news. This is shown when the text states 'his knees buckled beneath him', suggesting the news was overwhelming.
I think Marcus was upset because no one would be happy in that situation.
questions.explanation: Option C uses RACE: Restate ('Marcus felt devastated when he heard the news'), Answer ('devastated' — precise word), Cite (exact quote with quotation marks), Explain ('suggesting the news was overwhelming'). Options A, B, D miss two or more elements.

questions.question: For a language-use question on 'the wind howled like a wounded animal' (2 marks), which is the best answer?
options: The wind sounds like a wounded animal.
The writer uses a simile, comparing the wind to a wounded animal, to suggest the wind is both powerful and unsettling. This creates a sense of menace for the reader.
The wind is loud and scary.
questions.explanation: Language-use answers need TWO parts: name the technique (simile) AND explain the effect (creates menace). Option A names only; option B paraphrases; option D explains without naming.

questions.question: When the question is 'Do you agree that Daniel should have told his parents the truth?' (2 marks), the strongest answer:
options: Sits on the fence: 'Maybe yes, maybe no.'
Takes a clear stance and anchors it in text evidence ('a knot of guilt in his stomach').
Just states a personal opinion without any text reference.
Summarises what Daniel did in the story.
questions.explanation: Personal-response questions reward (1) a clear stance — never fence-sit, and (2) text evidence — your own reasoning must link back to the passage. Option B does both.

questions.question: For a vocab-in-context question on 'ravenous' (1 mark), which is the best answer?
options: Ravenous is what the children felt.
questions.explanation: Vocab-in-context answers must use a different word or short paraphrase. Option A defines with the same root (0 marks). Option C correctly substitutes a synonym that fits the context.

questions.question: A 2-part question asks: "What did the cleaner find, AND what did she do with it?" Which answer earns full marks?
options: The cleaner found a stack of football trading cards on a desk and threw them away with the rubbish.
questions.explanation: Two-part questions need BOTH halves answered. Option C addresses both 'what she found' (stack of cards) and 'what she did' (threw away with rubbish). Options A and B answer half each — capping at 1 mark.

cloze.title: Comprehension Cloze (15m)
cloze.blurb: 15 blanks, one word each. Four clue types: grammar, context, collocation, register.
keyQuestions: What part of speech does this blank need?
What context clues in the surrounding sentences narrow the choice?
Is there a collocation that fits (e.g., 'make' + a noun)?
Is my spelling correct? (Half the marks lost here are spelling.)

glossary.definition: A continuous passage with 15 blanks. Each blank takes exactly one word.
glossary.template: Use grammar + context + collocation clues together.

glossary.definition: Some blanks are decided by which word naturally pairs with surrounding words.
glossary.template: She made a _____ to volunteer. (decision) — verb 'make' + noun 'decision' is the natural pair.

glossary.definition: Using all four clue types TOGETHER to verify a single word, rather than guessing from one clue alone.
glossary.template: Grammar says 'noun'; Context says 'reaction to hunger'; Collocation says 'made a _____'; together they narrow to 'decision'.

cloze.learnHTML:
Comprehension Cloze — 15 marks

A passage of about 200 words with 15 blanks. Write ONE word in each blank. 1 mark per blank. Half the marks lost in this section are spelling errors — get the right word, then spell it perfectly.

The four clue types

  1. Grammar clue — the grammar structure tells you what part of speech is needed. 'She _____ very quickly' needs a verb.
  2. Context clue — the meaning of surrounding sentences narrows the choice. 'After the long hike, the children were _____ and could not wait to sit down to eat' needs a hungry-related word.
  3. Collocation clue — some blanks are decided by which word naturally pairs with surrounding words. 'She made a _____ to volunteer' wants 'decision' because 'make a decision' is the natural pair.
  4. Register clue — formal vs informal vocabulary fits the passage tone. A formal passage wants 'enquire' not 'ask'.

Trilateration — using all four clues together

Hard blanks are usually solved by triangulating multiple clues. Example: 'After the long hike, the children were _____ and could not wait to sit down to eat.'

  • Grammar: adjective (after 'were').
  • Context: physical reaction after hiking → tired or hungry.
  • Collocation: 'couldn't wait to eat' implies hunger.
  • Register: passage is narrative → 'famished' (literary) or 'ravenous' fit better than 'starving'.

Best answer: famished or ravenous.

The 4-step process

  1. Read the whole passage first, without filling anything (2 minutes). Understand the meaning and identify the tense.
  2. Fill the easy ones first, in order of confidence. Build momentum.
  3. Use clue triangulation for the hard ones: grammar + context + collocation often agree on one word.
  4. Check tense consistency: if the passage is past tense, your verb form must match.

High-frequency cloze collocation patterns (drill these)

  • burst into _____ (tears, laughter, flames)
  • heavy _____ (rain, traffic, meal, heart, workload)
  • make a _____ (decision, mistake, difference, effort)
  • do _____ (homework, research, business, a favour)
  • catch a _____ (cold, bus, glimpse)
  • had + past participle (had finished, had seen, had left — for two-past-event blanks)
  • on the brink of _____ (tears, collapse, discovery)

Spelling trap list (the 10 most-misspelt correct words)

believe · receive · separate · occurred · embarrass · necessary · tomorrow · definitely · restaurant · accommodation

Drill each 5 times during study. Use in a cloze-style sentence. Misspelling these costs the mark even when the word is right.
cloze.summaryHTML: Comprehension Cloze is 15 marks — constructed-response. The Collocations bank is the highest-leverage reference.
pslePrompts: Read the whole passage once before filling any blank.
Check tense consistency across the whole passage.
Spell every answer correctly — half the cloze marks lose to spelling.
When stuck, ask: what verb naturally goes with this noun?
Triangulate hard blanks using all four clue types — don't guess from one clue alone.

commonTraps.wrong: Writing two words in one blank: 'very cold' or 'has gone'.
commonTraps.fix: ONE word per blank. Pick the single best fit: 'freezing' or 'gone'.
commonTraps.why: Cloze rules allow only ONE word per blank. Two-word answers automatically score zero.

commonTraps.wrong: Using present tense in a past-tense passage: 'She runs to the door.' in a 'yesterday' passage.
commonTraps.fix: Identify the passage tense first. All verb blanks must match.
commonTraps.why: Tense inconsistency is one of the most common Cloze errors. Read the first paragraph to lock the tense before filling any verb blank.

commonTraps.wrong: Misspelling the correct word: 'beleive' instead of 'believe'.
commonTraps.fix: Double-check spelling of every answer before moving on. Drill the 10 high-frequency misspellings.
commonTraps.why: Half the lost Cloze marks are spelling errors on otherwise-correct words. Spell-checking is the highest-leverage habit.

commonTraps.wrong: Guessing from one clue: 'Grammar says noun, so I'll write _____.'
commonTraps.fix: Trilateration — use grammar + context + collocation together to narrow to one word.
commonTraps.why: Single-clue guessing works for easy blanks but fails on the hardest 3–4 each year. Those are where you lose marks.

commonTraps.wrong: Skipping the whole-passage read and diving in.
commonTraps.fix: Spend 2 minutes reading the whole passage WITHOUT filling anything. Understand the meaning and tense.
commonTraps.why: Many blanks depend on context two sentences away. Diving in without reading caps you at the easy blanks.

masteryCheckpoints: You read the entire passage first before filling in any blank.
You can identify the tense and maintain consistency throughout.
You know the 15 highest-frequency collocation patterns by heart (burst into, make a, heavy, catch a, etc.).
You spell your final answer correctly — even if the word is right, misspelling loses the mark.
You triangulate difficult blanks using all four clue types, not guessing from one clue alone.
You can fill 10 of the 15 blanks within 8 minutes, leaving time for the hard 5.

drillItems.prompt: Choose the best fill: "She _____ a decision to volunteer."
drillItems.explanation: 'Make a decision' is the natural verb-noun collocation. Cloze collocation clue.

drillItems.prompt: Choose the best fill: "When the news broke, she _____ into tears."
drillItems.explanation: 'Burst into tears' / 'burst into laughter' / 'burst into flames'. Fixed collocation.

drillItems.prompt: Choose the best fill: "After the long hike, the children were _____ and could not wait to eat."
drillItems.explanation: Context clue: long hike + waiting to eat → famished / ravenous (hungry to a literary degree).

drillItems.prompt: Choose the best fill: "By the time the bell rang, the students _____ already finished."
drillItems.explanation: Past perfect 'had' for the earlier of two past events. 'By the time' signal.

drillItems.prompt: Choose the best fill: "He suffers _____ a rare condition."
drillItems.explanation: Suffer FROM. Verb-preposition collocation, fixed pair.

drillItems.prompt: Choose the best fill: "They were on the _____ of giving up when the call came."
drillItems.explanation: 'On the brink of' giving up / collapse / tears. Standard fixed phrase.

drillItems.prompt: Choose the best fill: "_____ the heavy rain, the parade continued."
drillItems.explanation: Despite + noun phrase. The other options either need a clause or are incomplete.

drillItems.prompt: Choose the best fill: "It was a _____ opportunity she could not miss."
drillItems.explanation: 'Golden opportunity' is the fixed adjective-noun collocation.

drillItems.prompt: Spelling check: which is spelt correctly?
drillItems.explanation: I before E except after C → receive.

drillItems.prompt: Spelling check: which is spelt correctly?
drillItems.explanation: Separately has 'par' in the middle, not 'per'.

drillItems.prompt: Spelling check: which is spelt correctly?
drillItems.explanation: Accommodate has double c AND double m.

drillItems.prompt: Spelling check: which is spelt correctly?
drillItems.explanation: Embarrass has double r AND double s.

questions.question: In the cloze: 'After the long hike, the children were _____ and could not wait to sit down to eat', which clue type primarily decides the blank?
options: Context clue (the children's reaction to seeing food)
questions.explanation: The context clue (couldn't wait to eat after a long hike) tells you the children are hungry. A good fill is 'hungry', 'famished', 'ravenous', or 'exhausted-and-hungry'. Grammar gives you the part of speech, but context narrows the choice.

questions.question: In the cloze: 'She made a _____ to volunteer at the food bank', which collocation fits the blank?
questions.explanation: 'Make a decision' is the natural collocation. 'Make a thought' / 'make a wish' / 'make an idea' are unnatural (or have different meanings). This is a collocation-clue blank — verb 'make' demands its natural noun partner.

questions.question: The most common reason students lose marks on Comprehension Cloze is:
options: They write two words in one blank.
They spell their correct word incorrectly (e.g., 'beleive' for 'believe').
questions.explanation: Roughly half of the cloze marks lost are spelling errors on otherwise correct words. Double-check every word before writing it. Common misspellings: receive, believe, separately, occurred, embarrass, necessary, tomorrow, definitely.

questions.question: In the cloze: 'When the news broke, she _____ into tears.', which collocation completes the blank?
questions.explanation: 'Burst into tears' is the standard collocation (and 'burst into laughter', 'burst into flames'). This is a pure collocation-clue blank — the grammar allows several verbs, but only 'burst' pairs naturally with 'into tears'.

questions.question: Which is the strongest 4-step Cloze process?
options: Fill blanks in order; don't read the passage first.
Read the whole passage; fill easy ones first; triangulate hard ones; check tense.
Translate the passage to your first language, then fill.
questions.explanation: Read first (2 min), then fill the easy blanks to build momentum, then triangulate (grammar + context + collocation) for the hard ones, and check tense consistency throughout. This is the highest-yield strategy.

editing.title: Editing (10m)
editing.blurb: 10 lines with errors (~5 spelling, ~5 grammar). Some lines have NO error. Identify and correct.
keyQuestions: Is this line's error spelling or grammar?
If grammar, which of the six areas: tense, SVA, article, pronoun, adjective/adverb, preposition?
Am I sure my correction is spelled correctly?
Does this line have NO error? (Some lines don't.)

glossary.definition: Subject-Verb Agreement. Singular subject → singular verb; plural → plural.
Common error: 'its' vs 'it's'. 'It's' = it is; 'its' = belonging to it.
Words that sound the same but mean different things: their/there/they're, your/you're, to/too/two.

editing.learnHTML:
Editing — 10 marks

A passage of about 20–25 lines. Ten lines contain errors (approximately 5 spelling and 5 grammar). Some lines have NO error. Your task: identify the wrong word and write the correct word. 1 mark for correct identification AND correct spelling of the correction.

The six grammar areas to check (in order)

  1. Tense — is the verb form correct for the passage time?
  2. Subject-Verb Agreement — singular subject → singular verb.
  3. Article — a / an / the correct?
  4. Pronoun — right pronoun (I/me, he/him, who/whom)?
  5. Adjective / adverb — quick vs quickly?
  6. Preposition — depend ON, succeed IN, agree WITH.

Common spelling errors at PSLE level (drill these)

WrongRightMemory aid
recievereceivei before e except after c
beleivebelieve'lie' is inside believe
seperatelyseparately'par' not 'per' in the middle
occuredoccurreddouble r: oc-CUR-red
embarassembarrassdouble r AND double s
neccessarynecessary1 c, 2 s's
tommorrowtomorrow1 m, 2 r's: to-MOR-row
definatelydefinitelydef-IN-ite-ly (no 'a')
restarauntrestauranthas 'aura' inside
accomodateaccommodatedouble c AND double m

Homophone traps (the most-tested)

  • their / there / they're — possession / location / contraction of 'they are'.
  • your / you're — possession / contraction of 'you are'.
  • its / it's — possession / contraction of 'it is'.
  • to / too / two — preposition / 'also'+'excessive' / number 2.
  • write / right — verb 'to write' / direction or correctness.
  • here / hear — this place / to hear sound.

Irregular plural and verb forms

Plurals: child→children · man→men · woman→women · foot→feet · tooth→teeth · mouse→mice · person→people · sheep→sheep · goose→geese.

Past forms: go→went · eat→ate · run→ran · take→took · see→saw · write→wrote · break→broke · come→came · give→gave · know→knew · think→thought · buy→bought · catch→caught · teach→taught · bring→brought.
editing.summaryHTML: Editing is 10 marks. The six grammar areas: tense, SVA, article, pronoun, adjective/adverb, preposition. Spell every correction perfectly.
pslePrompts: Read the whole passage first to catch context-dependent errors.
Don't change correct words — only correct what is wrong.
Double-check your correction's spelling before writing it.
Watch the 'its' vs 'it's' apostrophe trap.
Some lines have NO error. Don't force a correction where none exists.

commonTraps.wrong: Changing a word that was actually correct.
commonTraps.fix: Only change a word if you are sure it is wrong. If unsure, leave it.
commonTraps.why: False positives lose marks the same as missed errors. Conservative editing is safer than aggressive editing.

commonTraps.wrong: Identifying the right error but spelling the fix wrong: 'recieve' for 'receive'.
commonTraps.fix: Drill the 10 high-frequency misspellings. Double-check before writing your correction.
commonTraps.why: The mark requires BOTH correct identification AND correct spelling. Either fail = zero on that line.

commonTraps.wrong: Forcing a correction on a line that has NO error.
commonTraps.fix: Accept that some lines have no error. Trust your read.
commonTraps.why: About 5 of the 20–25 lines have no error. Inventing problems where none exist loses marks.

commonTraps.wrong: 'Its' vs 'It's' confusion: 'The dog wagged it's tail.'
commonTraps.fix: 'It's' = it is. 'Its' = belonging to it. The dog wagged ITS tail. IT'S raining.
commonTraps.why: This is the single most-tested punctuation error in Editing.

commonTraps.wrong: Irregular plurals: 'The childrens were playing.'
commonTraps.fix: 'Children' is already plural. The CHILDREN were playing. Also: men, women, feet, teeth, mice.
commonTraps.why: Adding -s to already-plural irregular nouns is the most common SVA-adjacent error.

commonTraps.wrong: Diving in line-by-line without context.
commonTraps.fix: Read the WHOLE passage first to lock the tense and topic. Then go line-by-line.
commonTraps.why: Many errors are context-dependent (tense agreement across the passage; pronoun reference to earlier characters). Diving in without context misses these.

masteryCheckpoints: You read the entire passage first to understand context.
You identify the error type (spelling or grammar; if grammar, which of the six areas).
You spell your correction perfectly — double-check before writing.
You know the 10 most-tested spelling errors by heart and never make them.
You handle 'its' vs 'it's' correctly every time.
You leave 'no-error' lines alone — you don't force corrections.
You spot at least 8 of the 10 errors in a 25-line passage.

drillItems.prompt: Find the error: "The childrens were playing in the park."
drillItems.explanation: 'Children' is already the plural of 'child'. Other irregular plurals: men, women, feet, teeth, mice, people.

drillItems.prompt: Find the error: "The students walks to school every morning."
drillItems.explanation: Plural subject 'students' → plural verb 'walk' (no -s).

drillItems.prompt: Find the error: "She is a honest girl."
drillItems.explanation: 'Honest' starts with a vowel SOUND (silent h) → 'an honest'.

drillItems.prompt: Find the error: "Yesterday I goed to the market."
drillItems.explanation: Irregular past form: go → WENT (not goed). Other irregulars: eat→ate, run→ran, see→saw.

drillItems.prompt: Find the error: "Its raining heavily outside."
drillItems.explanation: It's = it is (contraction). Its = belonging to it. 'It's raining' = it is raining.

drillItems.prompt: Find the error: "He gave the book to Sarah and I."
drillItems.explanation: After a preposition (to), use object pronoun ME, not subject pronoun I. 'To Sarah and me'.

drillItems.prompt: Find the error: "She runned across the field as fast as she could."
drillItems.explanation: Irregular past form: run → RAN (not runned).

drillItems.prompt: Find the error in spelling: "I beleive he is right."
drillItems.explanation: Believe — 'lie' is inside believe. (I before E except after C.)

drillItems.prompt: Find the error: "The team are celebrating their win."
drillItems.explanation: Collective nouns (team, class, family) usually take singular in Singapore English. 'The team IS celebrating'.

drillItems.prompt: Find the error: "Each of the students have a textbook."
drillItems.explanation: 'Each of' takes a singular verb. 'Each of the students HAS a textbook'.

drillItems.prompt: Find the error: "She is more taller than her sister."
drillItems.explanation: Double comparative is wrong. Use 'taller' OR 'more tall' — never 'more taller'.

drillItems.prompt: Which line has NO error?
drillItems.explanation: Option B is correct: singular subject 'she' + singular verb 'enjoys'. A has SVA error; C has SVA error; D has 'their/they're' confusion.

questions.question: In the editing line 'The childrens were playing in the park', which is the error and correction?
questions.explanation: 'Children' is already the plural of 'child' — it is an irregular plural. Adding an 's' is wrong. Other irregular plurals: man/men, woman/women, foot/feet, tooth/teeth, mouse/mice, person/people.

questions.question: Which line contains an SVA (subject-verb agreement) error?
options: The students walks to school every morning.
The book is on the table.
questions.explanation: 'Students' is plural, so the verb should be 'walk' (not 'walks'). SVA rule: singular subject → singular verb (s on the end); plural subject → plural verb (no s). Options B, C, D are all correct.

questions.question: Editing scores 1 mark only when:
options: The correct line is identified AND the correction is spelled perfectly.
questions.explanation: Both halves must be right. Identifying the wrong word AND writing the correction with perfect spelling = 1 mark. A right error with a misspelled correction = 0 marks. Double-check spelling before writing.

questions.question: Which is the most-tested apostrophe error in Editing?
options: Forgetting apostrophes in possessive 's: Tom's book.
Using apostrophes in plurals: book's instead of books.
All of the above are equally tested.
questions.explanation: 'Its' vs 'it's' is THE most-tested apostrophe trap at PSLE. 'It's raining' (it is). 'The dog wagged its tail' (belonging to it). Memorise: apostrophe = contraction; no apostrophe = possession (for IT only).

questions.question: On a line that contains no error, the correct action is:
options: Force a correction somewhere; otherwise you get zero.
Leave the line alone and mark it 'no error' if the answer sheet allows.
Change the first word just in case.
questions.explanation: About 5 of the 20–25 lines have NO error. Forcing a correction loses the mark. Trust your read. The PSLE rewards accuracy — both finding errors AND leaving correct lines alone.

synthesis.title: Synthesis & Transformation (10m)
synthesis.blurb: 5 items × 2 marks. Combine or transform sentences while preserving meaning. Patterns: conjunctions, relative clauses, conditionals, direct↔reported, active↔passive, inversion.
keyQuestions: Which transformation pattern is this — conjunction, relative clause, conditional, speech, voice, inversion, despite/although?
Have I preserved the original meaning exactly?
Is my grammar perfect — tense, SVA, article all correct?
Have I started with the EXACT given word?

glossary.definition: Direct speech transformed to indirect: tense shifts back, pronouns change.
glossary.template: 'I am late,' she said. → She said that she _was_ late.

glossary.definition: Object becomes subject; agent introduced with 'by'.
glossary.template: The dog chased the cat. → The cat _was chased_ by the dog.

glossary.definition: Subject and auxiliary swap, used after 'Hardly', 'No sooner', 'Scarcely'.
glossary.template: Hardly had the bell rung when the students rushed out.

glossary.definition: After 'If' in hypothetical statements, use 'were' for all persons.
glossary.template: If I _were_ you, I would stay.

synthesis.learnHTML:
Synthesis & Transformation — 10 marks (5 × 2m)

Each item asks you to combine and/or transform sentences while preserving the original meaning. The highest density of grammar precision in the paper — one slip per item costs the full 2 marks.

The 8 high-frequency patterns (with worked examples)

  1. Conjunctions — He was tired. He kept working. → Although he was tired, he kept working.
  2. Relative clauses — The boy is my brother. He is wearing a red cap. → The boy who is wearing a red cap is my brother.
  3. If-conditionals — Eat your vegetables, or you won't grow tall. → If you don't eat your vegetables, you won't grow tall.
  4. Direct ↔ Reported speech — 'I am late,' she said. → She said that she was late. (Shift tense AND pronouns.)
  5. Active ↔ Passive — The dog chased the cat. → The cat was chased by the dog.
  6. Too…to ↔ So…that — He was too tired to walk. → He was so tired that he could not walk.
  7. Inversion (Hardly / No sooner / Scarcely) — As soon as the bell rang, the students rushed out. → Hardly had the bell rung when the students rushed out.
  8. Despite / Although — Although it was raining, the match continued. → Despite the rain, the match continued.

Three rules every S&T answer must follow

  1. Original meaning preserved. Do not add new information; do not soften or strengthen claims.
  2. Grammar perfect. No tense slip, no SVA error, no article error.
  3. Use the given word / starter exactly as instructed. If the prompt says 'Begin: Hardly had…', your sentence must start exactly with 'Hardly had'.

Reported speech — tense shift table

DirectReported
I am tiredshe was tired
I will goshe would go
I can doshe could do
I was doingshe had been doing
I have doneshe had done
I am working tomorrowshe was working the next day

Plus: shift pronouns (I → she/he), demonstratives (this → that), time markers (tomorrow → the next day).

Inversion pattern (Hardly / No sooner / Scarcely)

The structure is strictly: Hardly + had + subject + past participle + when + past simple.

  • As soon as the bell rang, the students rushed out. → Hardly had the bell rung when the students rushed out.
  • As soon as I sat down, the phone rang. → No sooner had I sat down than the phone rang.
  • As soon as the music started, the dancers entered. → Scarcely had the music started when the dancers entered.

Note: Hardly… when. No sooner… than. Scarcely… when. Don't mix the conjunctions.
synthesis.summaryHTML: Synthesis & Transformation is 10 marks (5 items × 2). Drill the 8 high-frequency patterns to automaticity.
pslePrompts: Preserve the original meaning exactly — no extra information.
If 'Begin: Hardly had...' or 'Use: despite', start with the exact required word.
Despite + noun phrase; Although + clause. Never mix.
Reported speech: shift tense AND pronouns AND time markers.
Hardly… when. No sooner… than. Scarcely… when. Don't mix conjunctions.

commonTraps.wrong: Although it rained, but the match continued.
commonTraps.fix: Pick ONE: 'Although it rained, the match continued' OR 'It rained, but the match continued'.
commonTraps.why: Double-up connectors are the most common S&T error. Although alone introduces the contrast — adding 'but' is redundant and ungrammatical.

commonTraps.wrong: Despite of the rain, the match continued. / Despite it was raining, the match continued.
commonTraps.fix: Despite + noun phrase (no 'of'): 'Despite the rain, the match continued.'
commonTraps.why: Despite NEVER takes 'of'. Despite NEVER takes a clause. The structure is fixed: Despite + noun.

commonTraps.wrong: Reported speech without tense shift: 'I am late,' she said → She said that she IS late.
commonTraps.fix: Shift the tense back one step: am → was, will → would, can → could, have → had.
commonTraps.why: Reported speech reports speech said earlier, so tense must shift back. Missing the shift is the #1 reported-speech error.

commonTraps.wrong: Reported speech without pronoun shift: 'I am late,' she said → She said that I was late.
commonTraps.fix: Shift the pronoun to match the speaker's perspective: I → she; my → her; we → they.
commonTraps.why: The reporter is no longer the speaker, so 'I' becomes 'she/he' in third-person reporting. Pronoun confusion is the #2 reported-speech error.

commonTraps.wrong: Hardly the bell had rung when the students rushed out.
commonTraps.fix: Hardly + HAD + subject + past participle: 'Hardly HAD the bell rung when the students rushed out.'
commonTraps.why: Inversion swaps the auxiliary BEFORE the subject. 'Hardly the bell had' has them in the wrong order.

commonTraps.wrong: Active → Passive without the 'by' agent: 'The papers were marked.' (when the question wanted the doer too)
commonTraps.fix: Include the agent: 'The papers were marked BY THE TEACHER.' Only omit 'by' if the doer is unknown or unimportant.
commonTraps.why: Passive must preserve who-did-what when the agent matters. The 'by' phrase is essential for full meaning.

masteryCheckpoints: You can identify which of the 8 patterns each question asks for in 10 seconds.
You preserve the original meaning exactly — no extra information, no softened or strengthened claims.
Your grammar is perfect: tense shifts correctly, SVA maintained, articles correct.
You use the given word / starter phrase EXACTLY as instructed.
You handle Despite (noun phrase) vs Although (clause) correctly every time.
Reported speech: you shift tense AND pronouns AND time markers in one move.
Inversion: aux + subject + past participle is automatic (Hardly HAD the bell rung).

drillItems.prompt: (Use: Despite) 'Although it was raining, the match continued.' becomes:
options: Despite it was raining, the match continued.
Despite of the rain, the match continued.
Despite the rain, the match continued.
Despite although it rained, the match continued.
drillItems.explanation: Despite + noun phrase (Despite the rain). Never 'of'. Never a clause.

drillItems.prompt: Convert to reported speech: '"I am late," she said.'
options: She said that she is late.
She said that she was late.
drillItems.explanation: Shift BOTH tense (am → was) AND pronoun (I → she).

drillItems.prompt: (Begin: Hardly had…) 'As soon as the bell rang, the students rushed out.'
options: Hardly the bell had rung when the students rushed out.
Hardly had the bell rung when the students rushed out.
Hardly did the bell rang when the students rushed out.
Hardly the bell rang when the students rushed out.
drillItems.explanation: Inversion: Hardly + had + subject + past participle + when + past simple.

drillItems.prompt: Combine with a relative clause: 'The girl is my sister. She is wearing yellow.'
options: The girl wearing yellow is my sister.
The girl who is wearing yellow is my sister.
My sister and the girl in yellow.
The girl is my sister, who is wearing yellow.
drillItems.explanation: Relative clause with 'who' joins the two sentences, identifying which girl.

drillItems.prompt: Active → Passive: 'The chef cooked the meal.' (Begin: The meal…)
options: The meal was cooked by the chef.
drillItems.explanation: Passive: object becomes subject; agent introduced with 'by'. Keep the doer.

drillItems.prompt: Convert to reported speech: '"I will go tomorrow," he said.'
options: He said that he will go tomorrow.
He said that he would go the next day.
He said I will go tomorrow.
drillItems.explanation: Shift tense (will → would) AND pronoun (I → he) AND time marker (tomorrow → the next day).

drillItems.prompt: (Begin: No sooner had…) 'I had just sat down when the phone rang.'
options: No sooner I had sat down than the phone rang.
No sooner had I sat down than the phone rang.
No sooner I sat down than the phone rang.
No sooner had I sat down when the phone rang.
drillItems.explanation: No sooner had + subject + past participle + THAN. Hardly… when. Scarcely… when. Different conjunctions.

drillItems.prompt: Too / So that: 'He was too tired to walk.' becomes:
options: He was so tired that he could walk.
He was so tired that he could not walk.
He was very tired so that he walked.
He was tired enough to walk.
drillItems.explanation: Too…to (cannot do) ↔ so…that (could NOT do). Preserve the negative meaning.

drillItems.prompt: Conditional: 'Eat your vegetables, or you won't grow tall.' becomes:
options: If you eat your vegetables, you won't grow tall.
If you don't eat your vegetables, you won't grow tall.
If you ate your vegetables, you wouldn't grow tall.
If you don't grow tall, eat your vegetables.
drillItems.explanation: Preserve the meaning: NOT eating vegetables → NOT growing tall. Type 1 conditional.

drillItems.prompt: Active → Passive: 'Many people read this book.'
options: This book is read by many people.
This book was read by many people.
drillItems.explanation: Active 'read' (present) → passive 'is read' (present). Tense matches the original.

drillItems.prompt: (Use: in spite of) 'Although he was scared, he spoke up.'
options: In spite he was scared, he spoke up.
In spite of he was scared, he spoke up.
In spite of his fear, he spoke up.
In spite of being scared, he spoke up — both 'in spite of his fear' AND 'in spite of being scared' work.
drillItems.explanation: In spite of + noun phrase OR gerund. Both options C and D structures are valid; D acknowledges both.

drillItems.prompt: Combine with 'although': 'He was tired. He kept working.'
options: He was tired but he kept working.
Although he was tired, he kept working.
Despite he was tired, he kept working.
He was tired, although he kept working.
drillItems.explanation: Although + clause. Place the contrast clause first for emphasis. Don't use both 'although' AND 'but'.

questions.question: Transform: 'Although it was raining heavily, the football match continued.' (Use: Despite)
options: Despite it was raining heavily, the football match continued.
Despite of the heavy rain, the football match continued.
Despite the heavy rain, the football match continued.
Despite raining heavily, the football match continued.
questions.explanation: 'Despite' takes a noun phrase, not a clause: 'Despite + noun'. So 'Although it was raining' (clause) becomes 'Despite the rain' (noun). Option A wrongly keeps the clause; option B adds 'of'; option D uses a gerund which is acceptable but B's structure is the standard PSLE answer.

questions.question: Convert to reported speech: '"I am late," she said.'
options: She said that she is late.
She said that she was late.
questions.explanation: Reported speech shifts BOTH tense AND pronouns: 'am → was', 'I → she'. Option B does both. Option A misses the tense shift; option C misses both; option D misses the pronoun shift.

questions.question: Transform: 'As soon as the bell rang, the students rushed out.' (Begin: Hardly had…)
options: Hardly the bell had rung when the students rushed out.
Hardly had the bell rung when the students rushed out.
Hardly did the bell rang when the students rushed out.
Hardly the bell rang when the students rushed out.
questions.explanation: Inversion after 'Hardly': aux + subject + past participle. The pattern is 'Hardly had + subject + past participle ... when + past simple'. Option B follows this exactly. Also: 'No sooner had X than Y' / 'Scarcely had X when Y'.

questions.question: Combine using a relative clause: 'The girl is my sister. She is wearing a yellow dress.'
options: The girl wearing yellow dress is my sister.
The girl who is wearing a yellow dress is my sister.
The girl is my sister and she wear a yellow dress.
My sister and the girl in the yellow dress.
questions.explanation: A relative clause joins two sentences by replacing the repeated subject ('She') with 'who'. Option B preserves the article ('a'), the verb form ('is wearing'), and clarifies which girl. Option A drops the article.

questions.question: Transform: 'The chef cooked the meal.' (Begin: The meal…)
options: The meal was cooked by the chef.
questions.explanation: Active → Passive: the object ('the meal') becomes the subject; the agent ('by the chef') is introduced with 'by'. Option B includes the agent — necessary when the doer matters. Option A omits the agent, which loses the meaning that the chef did it.

visual-text.title: Visual Text Comprehension (5m)
visual-text.blurb: 5 MCQ items on a poster, infographic, advertisement, or webpage. Tests literal, inferential, language-use, and purpose understanding.
keyQuestions: Is this question literal, inferential, language-use, or purpose?
Have I read ALL text including small print and captions?
Which option is most specifically supported by the visual?
Have I checked for the 'almost-right' distractor (changes one detail)?

glossary.definition: A wrong MCQ option that changes only one detail (a number, a name, a date). The most common trap.
Purpose = what the visual tries to DO; audience = who it speaks TO. Don't confuse them.
glossary.template: An NEA litter poster's purpose is to deter littering; its audience is the general public.

glossary.definition: Typography, colour, layout, image style — all carry meaning. BLOCK LETTERS = urgency; red = warning; cartoon style = family-friendly.
glossary.template: Block letters + red banner = enforcement message.

visual-text.learnHTML:
Visual Text Comprehension — 5 marks (Booklet A MCQ)

A non-prose text like a poster, infographic, advertisement, brochure, or webpage screenshot. The text combines images, headings, bullet points, and short paragraphs. 5 MCQ items × 1 mark each.

The four question types

  1. Literal — find the information. Compare options word-for-word against the visual.
  2. Inferential — what does the visual suggest about the audience or context?
  3. Language-use — why this word / phrase / typography choice? What effect does it create?
  4. Purpose / audience — what is the visual trying to do? Who is it speaking to?

The four-step strategy

  1. Read everything on the visual (1–2 min). All text, including small print, captions, slogans.
  2. Identify the structure. What sections? What does the image contribute?
  3. Read each MCQ stem and underline key words. 'According to' = literal; 'why does the writer' = language-use; 'what can you infer' = inferential.
  4. MCQ elimination. Cross out clearly wrong options. Of the remaining two, pick the one most specifically supported by the visual.

Singaporean visual-text decoder (5 genre signatures)

GenreVisual signatureExpected audience / purpose
HPB / MOH postersCartoon style, bright primary colours, family/children visible.Families with children. Purpose: behaviour change (wellness, habits).
NEA postersBlock letters, red banners, void-deck or street placement, fines mentioned.General public. Purpose: enforcement / deterrence.
LTA safetyHigh-contrast paired photos (safe vs unsafe), dusk/night lighting, reflective clothing.Pedestrians, especially children. Purpose: safety behaviour.
SCDF emergencyNumbered steps, directive verbs (NEVER, do NOT), emergency numbers.General public. Purpose: emergency procedural clarity.
NLB community flyersBright photography, children/families, metaphorical imagery (books floating), call-to-action.Families. Purpose: open up possibility / participation.

The 'almost-right distractor' trap

Distractors change ONE detail — a number, a name, a date, or a phrasing. If the visual says 'fines start at $300', expect distractors that say '$30' or 'fines of $300' (no 'start at'). Read every option word-for-word against the visual.

Worked example. Visual: 'Fines start at $300. Repeat offenders may receive a Corrective Work Order.'

Question: 'What is the minimum fine for littering?'

  • A. $30 — almost-right distractor (changes one digit).
  • B. $3000 — almost-right (changes one digit).
  • C. $300 — correct (matches the visual).
  • D. Fines are not stated — wrong; they are stated.

Language-use effect vocabulary

  • Block letters / CAPS = urgency, seriousness, attention.
  • Bright colours = energy, excitement, hope.
  • Dark / muted colours = somberness, caution, concern.
  • Paired images (good/bad) = contrast, choice, consequence.
  • Floating / scattered imagery = freedom, possibility, exploration.
  • Numbered steps = clarity, procedural safety, control.
  • Short, balanced phrases (e.g., 'See. Be Seen.') = memorability, slogan effect.
visual-text.summaryHTML: Visual Text is 5 marks (MCQ in Booklet A in the 2025+ format). 6 worked Singaporean examples in VisualText_Examples_KB.md.
pslePrompts: Read every word on the visual, including small print.
Compare each option word-for-word against the visual.
Block letters and bright colours are usually about urgency/attention — name the effect.
Don't confuse purpose with audience.
Identify the genre (HPB/NEA/LTA/SCDF/NLB) in 10 seconds — it guides your inference.

commonTraps.wrong: Reading only the headline and skipping captions / small print.
commonTraps.fix: Read EVERY word on the visual: headlines, sub-headers, captions, small print, slogans.
commonTraps.why: Distractors often hide in detail (e.g., 'fines start at $300' becomes 'fines of $300' in a distractor). The small print is fair game.

commonTraps.wrong: Picking the 'almost-right' distractor that uses familiar words: option says '$30' when the visual says '$300'.
commonTraps.fix: Compare every option word-for-word against the visual. Watch for numbers, dates, names changed by one detail.
commonTraps.why: This is the #1 trap pattern in Visual Text MCQ. Almost-right distractors look correct until you compare carefully.

commonTraps.wrong: Confusing purpose with audience: 'Purpose is to inform parents' (that's audience).
commonTraps.fix: Purpose = what the visual DOES (persuade, warn, inform, instruct). Audience = WHO it speaks to.
commonTraps.why: Both are common question types. Mixing them up loses both marks.

commonTraps.wrong: Ignoring design choices: typography, colour, image style.
commonTraps.fix: Block letters = urgency. Red = warning. Cartoon = family-friendly. Use these design signals to support inference.
commonTraps.why: Language-use questions test design analysis. The visual's choices carry meaning you must name and explain.

commonTraps.wrong: Picking the option that 'sounds best' without supporting evidence.
commonTraps.fix: Pick the option most specifically supported by visible evidence in the visual.
commonTraps.why: MCQ rewards precision, not plausibility. Always anchor your choice in something you can point to.

masteryCheckpoints: You can identify the Singapore genre (HPB / NEA / LTA / SCDF / NLB) in 10 seconds from design alone.
You read ALL text including small print and captions before answering.
You compare each MCQ option word-for-word against the visual (catching the 'almost-right' distractor).
You can explain why a design choice (block letters, paired photos, floating imagery) creates a specific effect.
You distinguish purpose (what the visual tries to do) from audience (who it speaks to).
You eliminate clearly wrong options first, then choose between the remaining two by specificity.

drillItems.prompt: An NEA poster reads 'Fines start at $300. Repeat offenders may receive a Corrective Work Order.' Which is the 'almost-right' distractor?
drillItems.explanation: Almost-right distractor changes ONE detail — a digit. Always compare options word-for-word.

drillItems.prompt: A library flyer reads 'Borrow a book. Borrow a world.' The two-phrase headline is used to:
options: Suggest borrowing a book opens up bigger experiences.
Indicate the library has two sections.
drillItems.explanation: Language-use question — short paired phrases suggest transformation.

drillItems.prompt: An HPB poster in a hawker centre uses cartoon imagery + simple bullet points. The audience is:
drillItems.explanation: Inferential — design choices (cartoon + simple language + hawker placement) point to families.

drillItems.prompt: An SCDF infographic uses NUMBERED STEPS + 'NEVER' in capitals. Why?
options: Numbered steps signal procedural order; 'NEVER' in caps signals an absolute rule.
drillItems.explanation: Two effects in one answer: order (numbered) + prohibition (caps).

drillItems.prompt: An LTA poster shows a paired photo: one child in dark clothes (faint), one in reflective sash (visible). The technique is:
options: Contrast — shows safe vs unsafe as a choice.
drillItems.explanation: Paired images = contrast technique. Makes the choice concrete.

drillItems.prompt: Question stem: 'According to the poster…' — what question type is this?
drillItems.explanation: 'According to' = literal. Find the exact words in the visual.

drillItems.prompt: Question stem: 'What can you infer about the audience?' — what question type?
drillItems.explanation: 'What can you infer' = inferential. Read design choices to infer audience or context.

drillItems.prompt: Question stem: 'Why does the writer use the phrase…?' — what question type?
drillItems.explanation: 'Why does the writer use…' = language-use. Name the technique + explain the effect.

drillItems.prompt: An NLB flyer shows floating book-covers like islands. The visual metaphor suggests:
options: Books open up new worlds and possibilities.
drillItems.explanation: Floating / scattered imagery = freedom, possibility, exploration. Language-use effect.

drillItems.prompt: A poster says 'Don't miss out!' Why does the writer use this phrase?
options: To create urgency and fear of missing something valuable.
drillItems.explanation: Urgency + scarcity language drives immediate action. Common advertising technique.

questions.question: An NEA poster says 'Fines start at $300. Repeat offenders may receive a Corrective Work Order.' Which is the most likely 'almost-right' distractor in a literal MCQ?
questions.explanation: The 'almost-right' distractor changes ONE detail — typically a number. Option C changes $300 to $30. The other distractors change phrasing in subtler ways. Always compare every option word-for-word against the visual.

questions.question: A library flyer reads 'Borrow a book. Borrow a world.' The two-phrase headline is used to:
options: Suggest that borrowing a book opens up much bigger experiences and possibilities.
Indicate the library has two sections.
Match the title of a popular movie.
questions.explanation: This is a language-use question. The paired short phrases are deliberate — they suggest a transformation (book → world). The technique creates association in the reader's mind: a book is more than itself.

questions.question: An HPB poster in a hawker centre uses bright cartoon imagery and simple bullet points. Its most likely audience is:
options: Adults working long hours in offices
questions.explanation: Inferential question — the audience is read from design choices. Cartoon style + simple language + hawker-centre placement all point to families with children. Audience answers ask 'who is this designed FOR?', not 'what does it DO?' (that's purpose).

questions.question: A SCDF infographic uses NUMBERED STEPS and the word 'NEVER' in capitals. Why?
options: Numbered steps signal procedural clarity (the order matters in an emergency); 'NEVER' in capitals signals an absolute rule that must not be broken.
Because emergency posters are always in capitals.
questions.explanation: Language-use question. The design choices carry specific meaning: numbers convey order/procedure (critical in emergencies); capitalised 'NEVER' conveys an absolute rule (a stronger prohibition than lowercase 'never'). Option C names BOTH effects.

questions.question: An LTA poster shows a paired photograph: one child in dark clothes walking at dusk (barely visible), another in a reflective sash (clearly visible). The technique is:
options: Contrast — visually demonstrates safe vs unsafe behaviour as a choice.
Mystery — to make the reader curious.
Decoration — to make the poster pretty.
questions.explanation: Paired images are a deliberate contrast technique. They show two outcomes side-by-side, making the choice concrete: 'this could be you, or this could be you.' LTA safety posters use this pattern frequently.
